metadata.title: The Bronze Argument: How a Statue Makes a Political Claim
metadata.purpose: vox-explainer slate cut of Writing Guide candidate 10. Source chapter: writing-guide/chapters/20-image-analysis-what-you-see.md (Roosevelt statue compositional hierarchy / image-analysis mechanism only).
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL (plain ink) = the analytical reading — what the composition argues, what the formal choices do; CRIMSON = the naive surface reading — what a label-free glance sees without analysis; GOLD = single highlighter fill, once per graphic
metadata.note: Card exclusions honored: NO full visual-elements vocabulary (no color, texture, light analysis beyond what composition requires); NO Demuth Dancing Sailors analysis; NO AI image-generation complications. One mechanism only: visual composition as argument — elevation, anonymity, and arrangement encode hierarchy; close-reading discipline applies to images the same way it applies to prose. Invented example numbers labeled illustrative in FACTCHECK.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

skin_warnings: B01: palette=claude but the cold open is 'un-annotated' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
B13: palette=claude but the outro is 'un-annotated' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. For eighty-one years, the main entrance of a major American museum was framed by an argument. Nobody had to read it. The composition made it.

card.sub: how a statue makes a political claim

beats.narration_text: This is the Theodore Roosevelt equestrian statue at the American Museum of Natural History. Installed in 1940. Removed in 2020.
beats.scene_description: Archival photograph of the Theodore Roosevelt equestrian statue at AMNH, full three-figure composition visible — Roosevelt mounted on horseback, two figures flanking on foot
beats.new_visual_element: [STILL] Roosevelt statue at AMNH — the three-figure composition

build.needs: YOU → drop a historical image at media/B02.png (or .mp4)
build.suggested: find: [STILL] Roosevelt statue at AMNH — the three-figure composition

beats.narration_text: Roosevelt sits mounted at the center. A Native American man walks beside the horse on one side. An African man walks beside the horse on the other. Neither is named. Neither is mounted.

production_viz.label: three-figure composition — reveal
production_viz.mechanic: three stick-figure silhouettes appear: Roosevelt elevated and centered (on horse outline), two unnamed figures lateral and below on foot; elevation lines drawn from each figure to a shared baseline; label 'named' appears over Roosevelt, question marks over the others

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] three-figure composition diagram — elevation and naming

beats.narration_text: How does a visual composition make an argument — and what does it mean to analyze a statue the way you would analyze a sentence?

card.copy: How does composition make an argument?

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CARD] THE QUESTION — composition as argument

beats.narration_text: Most people look at a statue and see a subject. A historical figure. They see what is depicted. They do not yet see what the depiction is doing.

production_viz.label: naive reading vs analysis
production_viz.mechanic: two-panel layout: left panel chip 'what you see' with a simple figure silhouette; right panel chip 'what it argues' with an arrow showing composition-as-claim — the right panel starts blank and fills in later

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] what you see vs. what it argues — two-panel setup

beats.narration_text: Image analysis is the close-reading discipline applied to visuals. Three layers: description — what is in the frame. Reflection — what it evokes. Analysis — what formal choices produce the effect.

production_viz.label: three layers of image analysis
production_viz.mechanic: three stacked horizontal bands appear in sequence: DESCRIPTION (ink), REFLECTION (ink), ANALYSIS (teal) — each with a short serif label; ANALYSIS is the top layer and arrives last, with a subtle raise-up motion

beats.narration_text: Now read the Roosevelt composition as argument. The camera is the reader. It moves from the ground up — from the unnamed figures at eye level to the mounted center above.

production_viz.label: scan upward — the hierarchy read bottom to top
production_viz.mechanic: the three-figure diagram from B03 reappears; a camera-eye indicator starts at the two lateral figures and pans upward to Roosevelt; elevation lines animate with arrows pointing up; the scan traces the hierarchy as movement

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] scan animation — camera traces hierarchy upward

beats.narration_text: Three formal choices encode the argument. Elevation: Roosevelt above. Naming: Roosevelt labeled, the others anonymous. Arrangement: Roosevelt centered, the others lateral. These are not neutral facts about the bronze.

production_viz.label: three formal choices — elevation, naming, arrangement
production_viz.mechanic: three label chips appear beside the three-figure diagram, one at a time: ELEVATION (with arrow up to Roosevelt), NAMING (Roosevelt labeled vs. question marks), ARRANGEMENT (center vs. lateral brackets) — each chip lands beside its figure annotation

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] three annotation chips — ELEVATION, NAMING, ARRANGEMENT

beats.narration_text: The same argumentative moves that work in prose work in bronze. Who gets named and who gets abstracted. What is foregrounded and what is peripheral. The composition is the argument.

production_viz.label: prose moves = bronze moves
production_viz.mechanic: two-column comparison: left column 'IN PROSE' with text labels (named/abstracted, foregrounded/peripheral); right column 'IN BRONZE' with corresponding visual-composition labels; a center divider line between them; both columns fill in together

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] prose moves vs. bronze moves — parallel structure

beats.narration_text: The removal in 2020 does not close the analysis. The empty plinth is its own image. An absence is also a composed visual fact — and it is also an argument.
beats.scene_description: Photograph of the empty plinth at AMNH after the statue's removal, 2020 — the blank pedestal in context of the museum entrance
beats.new_visual_element: [STILL] empty plinth — the absence as argument

build.needs: YOU → drop a historical image at media/B10.png (or .mp4)
build.suggested: find: [STILL] empty plinth — the absence as argument

beats.narration_text: Here is how the analysis works end to end. Named figure on horseback, elevated, centered: the composition encodes dominance. Two unnamed figures on foot, lateral, below eye level: the composition encodes subordination. Neither label appears in the inscription. Both arguments are in the bronze. [illustrative]

production_viz.label: the argument read end to end
production_viz.mechanic: the three-figure diagram with two columns of annotation: formal choice on left, argument produced on right; each pair appears together — elevated+centered / DOMINANCE; lateral+unnamed / SUBORDINATION; arrows connect formal choice to argument claim

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] formal choice to argument claim — annotated diagram

beats.narration_text: When you analyze an image: start with description — name what is visible, not what it means. Then ask what the formal choices are doing. Elevation, naming, arrangement — any of these can be the argument. The composition tells you where to look.

production_viz.label: the practice — three diagnostic questions
production_viz.mechanic: three serif question labels appear in sequence as a vertical list: 'What is visible?' (description), 'What do formal choices do?' (analysis), 'Where is the argument?' (synthesis) — each appears as the narration names it, with a small ink marker beside each

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] three diagnostic questions — the analytical sequence

beats.narration_text: How does composition make an argument? The same way a sentence does. By choosing what to name, what to elevate, and what to leave anonymous. The argument is in the arrangement.

card.copy: The argument is in the arrangement.

beats.new_visual_element: [CARD] endcard — the argument is in the arrangement

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CARD] endcard — the argument is in the arrangement
